text: This is Dr. Matthew St. Laurent and today I will be presenting a laparoscopic loop duodenal switch, a modification of the BPD with duodenal switch.

The surgeon inserts a five millimeter optical trocar on the left side to insufflate the abdominal cavity with carbon dioxide, creating a visible working space. Subsequently, additional trocars are introduced in standard positions, employing a five trocar technique similar to that used in sleeve gastrectomy procedures.

The Nathanson liver retractor is introduced to elevate the left lobe of the liver, exposing the upper portion of the stomach. The surgeon identifies the ileocecal valve and follows the ileum upstream for 250 to 300 centimeters to select a loop of small bowel for anastomosis to the duodenum. This loop is tacked to the upper portion of the greater omentum using a simple tacking suture for easy identification later in the procedure.

The surgeon begins the sleeve gastrectomy by dissecting the lower portion of the stomach using ultrasonic shears. The epiploic vessels along the lateral surface are taken down, gaining access to the retrogastric space. The dissection continues upstream, dividing short gastric vessels with ultrasonic shears to control bleeding.

The surgeon continues dissection upstream, separating soft adhesions between the posterior stomach and pancreas to mobilize the stomach for sleeve gastrectomy. The spleen is visible on the right side of the screen as attachments between the stomach and spleen are carefully taken down to avoid bleeding. The dissection progresses superiorly, exposing the left diaphragm and taking down phrenoesophageal attachments to mobilize the esophagus circumferentially. Dissection in the retroesophageal space begins, with the left diaphragmatic edge visible.

The surgeon rotates the stomach and opens the right side of the omental window, proceeding towards the right crus of the diaphragm. The phrenoesophageal attachments are carefully dissected to mobilize the esophagus away from the diaphragmatic edge. An instrument on the left holds the right crus of the diaphragm during this dissection.

The surgeon continues dissection in the posterior space, creating a retrogastric window and inserting an articulating banding instrument to elevate the esophagus for better counter traction. The posterior vagus nerve is visible and preserved during dissection. Loose areolar tissue is removed to expose the columns of the diaphragm, including the right and left cruce and the aorta. The esophagus and stomach are dissected away from the diaphragm, and a posterior repair of the diaphragm is performed using non-absorbable sutures in a figure of eight pattern to close the diaphragmatic defect and prevent gastric pouch migration.

The surgeon locates three to four centimeters of the esophagus below the diaphragm, ensuring the lower esophageal sphincter is positioned optimally. Care is taken not to close the diaphragmatic defect too tightly to prevent postoperative swallowing difficulties and reflux.

The surgeon completes the closure of the diaphragmatic defect and proceeds with the dissection of the lower stomach using ultrasonic shears. The epiploic vessels along the lateral border of the stomach are taken down, and the loose areolar tissue is dissected to free the distal stomach. The dissection extends to include four to five centimeters of the duodenum.

The distal stomach is fully mobilized, with dissection extending at least four centimeters beyond the pylorus. An articulating banding instrument is used to mobilize the first portion of the duodenum. The pylorus is visible, and the resection for sleeve gastrectomy begins approximately one to two centimeters proximal to the pylorus. The first two firings of the stapler are performed without a calibrating tube to prevent kinking or twisting of the distal stomach.

The surgeon ensures not to narrow the sharp angulation at the lower border of the stomach. After the third firing of the stapler is applied and clamped on the stomach, a 42 French bougie calibrating tube is inserted into the distal stomach to assist in forming the appropriate size pouch.

The surgeon places the calibrating tube and fires the third stapler to continue the resection along the vertical portion of the sleeve, closely hugging the tube for optimal postoperative restriction. The resection progresses up to the junction of the esophagus and stomach, ensuring staples are not placed on the esophagus to prevent leaks. The tissue reinforcement strip is divided, and the specimen is removed from the abdominal cavity.

After extracting the specimen, the surgeon reintroduces the trocar. The gastric staple line is inspected for hemostasis, and the bougie calibrating tube is removed. The pylorus is visible, and the surgeon proceeds with resecting the duodenum just distal to the pylorus. Following the division of the duodenum, the duodenal ileal anastomosis is initiated.

The surgeon re-identifies the loop of ileum and divides the fixation suture. The loop of small bowel is brought up to the duodenum for anastomosis. A hand-sewn two-layer anastomosis is performed, starting with a posterior row of running 2-0 silk suture along the duodenum's width to create a wide opening. The suture begins at the superior aspect of the duodenum and incorporates the entire ileal loop. After completing the posterior layer, enterotomies are made in both the duodenum and ileum distally, and the inner row of sutures is formed.

The surgeon uses an absorbable three-oh vicryl suture to form the inner row of the anastomosis, running the suture from top to bottom along the entire length. After completing the posterior inner row, the anterior inner row is formed using the same type of suture, visibly completing the anterior row of the anastomosis.

The surgeon completes the anterior outer row of sutures using a non-absorbable two-oh silk suture in a running fashion, closing the outer layer of the anastomosis from the top to the bottom. The completed two-layer closure of the anastomosis is visibly wide, reducing the risk of stricture formation.

The surgeon performs an upper endoscopy to evaluate the staple line and anastomosis, placing the anastomosis under a saline bath while advancing the endoscope through the efferent and afferent limbs. An air insufflation test is conducted to check for leaks, confirming hemostasis along the staple line. The anastomosis is reinforced with fibrin sealant, and a Jackson Pratt drain is inserted along the lower border of the stomach to the apex of the spleen to aid in removing leftover abdominal irrigation and checking for leaks postoperatively. The procedure concludes with the beginning of abdominal closure.

The surgeon removes the larger trocars and closes the fascial defects using a suture needle passer with absorbable ovicryl sutures, aiming to reduce the risk of postoperative hernia. The smaller trocars remain in place as they pose minimal risk of hernia, thus requiring no closure.
The surgeon aspirates the remaining air from the abdominal cavity, marking the completion of the abdominal operation.
